title: Stability Thresholds for Gravitationally Induced Entanglement in Shielded Setups
authors: Jan Bulling, Marit O. E. Steiner, Julen S. Pedernales, Martin B. Plenio
abstract: Proposed experiments for gravitationally induced entanglement (GIE) typically suppress direct electromagnetic interactions between two massive particles by inserting a conducting Faraday shield. For superconducting particles, their large diamagnetism requires additional magnetic shielding to screen magnetic dipolar interactions. Here, we analyze the effect of residual particle-shield interactions and show that both Casimir and magnetic-dipole interactions can severely limit GIE tests by imprinting large phases. We quantify how run-to-run positional and orientational fluctuations of the setup elements, including the shield, trapping potentials, and detectors, convert these phases into effective decoherence, strongly reducing the detectable bipartite entanglement. In particular, we show that magnetic interactions between the particles and a superconducting shield constitute a major noise source, especially relevant for levitated superconducting particles. Treating the vibrational modes of the shield quantum mechanically, we further find that thermal vibrations generate persistent particle-shield correlations and can even mediate particle-particle entanglement that can mimic a gravitational signal. Finally, we derive quantitative thresholds on the maximum tolerable positional and orientational fluctuations of the setup elements required to observe entanglement, and propose mitigation strategies including geometry optimization and shield cooling to preserve a genuine GIE signature.
summary: This paper analyzes the feasibility of detecting gravitationally induced entanglement between two massive particles in the presence of a physical shield. It demonstrates that while shields are necessary to suppress electromagnetic crosstalk, they introduce new noise sources through Casimir/magnetic interactions and mechanical vibrations. The authors quantify the extreme precision required for positioning and orientation to prevent these effects from masking the gravitational signal. Ultimately, they provide specific stability thresholds and suggest strategies like shield cooling to preserve entanglement.
main_problem: Investigating the stability of gravitationally induced entanglement (GIE) against decoherence caused by residual electromagnetic interactions (Casimir and magnetic-dipole) and setup fluctuations in shielded experimental setups.
main_result: The study identifies that run-to-run fluctuations in the shield, traps, and detectors convert residual electromagnetic phases into effective decoherence, and that thermal vibrations of the shield can mimic gravitational entanglement signals.
method: The authors use a quantum mechanical treatment of shield vibrational modes, a Lindblad master equation for Gaussian noise, and second-order expansions of the gravitational and electromagnetic Hamiltonians.
model_or_system: The system consists of two massive, spatially delocalized nanoparticles (either silica or superconducting lead) separated by a distance 2L, placed near a conducting or superconducting circular shield.
key_observables: Bipartable entanglement (logarithmic negativity), entanglement rate, phase accumulation, and decoherence rates.
important_parameters: Particle mass (10^-11 kg), particle radius (10 micrometers), shield thickness, separation distance, and thermal occupation of shield modes.
main_assumptions: The particles are assumed to be electrically neutral; the gravitational potential is expanded to second order; and the Proximity-Force Approximation (PFA) is used for Casimir interactions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup schematic; Figure 2 illustrates types of geometric variations; Figure 3 shows entanglement evolution and sensitivity; Figure 13 compares Casimir interaction models; Figure 15 shows critical angular variation thresholds; Figure 16 shows entanglement dynamics with single vs. multi-mode shield models.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the GIE setup and electromagnetic threats, proceeds to quantify how geometric fluctuations lead to decoherence, analyzes the impact of thermal shield vibrations, and concludes with derived stability thresholds and mitigation strategies.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it provides a rigorous analysis of how environmental noise and mechanical fluctuations in a controlled setup lead to decoherence and the emergence of non-classical correlations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Setup and initial states. a, Schematic illustration of an idealized setup for gravitationally induced entangle- ment (GIE) experiments considered in this work. Two particles A and B of radius R are prepared in spatially delocalized quantum states and positioned at a fixed center-to-center separation 2L. A circular shield of thickness ds and radius rs is placed midway between the particles to suppress unwanted electromagnetic crosstalk while preserving the gravitational coupling. The angles θA/B indicate the orientation of each particle’s principal delocalization axis (here without loss of generality the x-direction) and the shield. Two detectors measure the state of the particles...

FIG. 2: Geometry variations a, Uncertainty in the preparation of the initial state. The initial state ρ(0) = R dξ p(x) |ψ(t)⟩⟨ψ(t)| is mixed. The time evolution does not generate additional decoherence and any loss of purity is solely inherited from the initial state. b, Run-to-run variations of the setup. The varying geometry results in run- dependent time evolution operators Uξ(t) which generate, after averaging over ξ, decoherence even if the initial state ρ0 is pure. We distinguish the two experimentally relevant cases of distance ξ(L) and orientation ξ(θ) variations in the orientation and placement of the 1. shield and 2. detectors/traps discussed in the text.

FIG. 3: Entanglement between Gaussian squeezed states subject to shield variations (see Fig. 2(b)1.) a, Time evolution of the logarithmic negativity EN for Gaussian wave packets in the presence of particle–shield magnetic dipolar interactions in the parallel (θ = 0, top) and linear (θ = π/2, bottom) orientation. The parallel configuration is illustrated with run-to-run angular fluctuations of the shield orientation, while the linear orientation is shown with variations in the lateral shield placement. The principal x-mode is initially in a thermal state with occupation number ¯nx. Time is expressed in units of t0 = ℏ/(λ∆x) with the gravitational coupling strength λ = GMAMB/(4L3),...

FIG. 4: Entanglement between Gaussian squeezed states subject to detector and trap variations (see Fig. 2(b)2.) a, Time evolution of the logarithmic negativity EN for Gaussian wave packets in presence of particle– shield magnetic dipolar interactions in the parallel (top) and linear (bottom) orientations. The parallel configuration is illustrated with angular fluctuations of the detectors over many runs, while the linear configuration is shown with variations in the detector positioning. After saturation, the entanglement decays much faster back to zero compared to the previous case. b, Logarithmic negativity at time t = t0π/4 as a function of angular variations ∆θ for both orientations and...

FIG. 5: Entanglement for two-level cat-states sub- ject to shield variations (see Fig. 2(b)1.) Logarith- mic negativity in the parallel and linear orientation after evolution times t = t0π/4, corresponding to t ≈129 ms for silica particles and t ≈7 ms for superconducting lead. We use the parameters from Tab. I. top, Entanglement as a function of fluctuations in the shield orientation. In gray we show the first-order analytical expressions derived in Apx. D 2. bottom, Entanglement as a function of fluc- tuations in the particle–shield separation. The qualitative dependence on orientation and type of the variation closely mirrors the behavior for Gaussian states in Fig. 3.

FIG. 6: Entanglement for two-level cat-states subject to detector and trap variations (Fig. 2(b)2.) Loga- rithmic negativity in the parallel and linear orientation after evolution times t = t0π/4. We use the parameters from Tab. I. a, Entanglement as a function of angular variations with the first-order analytical expressions of Eq. (11) shown in gray. b, Entanglement as a function of fluctuations in the particle–shield separation in analogy to Fig. 4(c). c, Entanglement for combined variations ξ(L) A/B and ξ(θ) A/B in both discussed orientations (black outlines) after an evolution time of t = t0π/4. In the white region, the logarithmic negativity is zero.

FIG. 7: Entanglement dynamics for continuous cat-states. a, Logarithmic negativity EN for continuous initial states interpolating between squeezed Gaussian states and two-level cat-states, computed numerically in a truncated Fock basis at zero temperature. Results are shown for the parallel (top) and linear (bottom) orientations with variations in detector positioning. For reference, the corresponding analytical solutions for Gaussian and two-level cat-states with identical position variance (∆x)2 are shown in black. The initial linear growth of entanglement with rate Γent. = ∂tEN|t=0 is identical across the family of states; in agreement with Ref. [26]. b, Continuous cat-state in analogy...

FIG. 8: Entanglement in the presence of quasi-static trap fluctuations. a, Time evolution of the logarithmic negativity EN for magnetically levitated superconducting lead particles in an harmonic trap (ω = 100×2π Hz) in the linear configuration. Time is expressed in units of t0 = ℏ/(λ∆x2) with the gravitational coupling strength λ, corresponding to t0 ≈9 ms for the superconducting lead particles shown here. Periodic oscillations arise from the harmonic motion of the trapped particles. Different run-to-run fluctuations ∆L of the trap center are included. Nonzero entanglement is observable only near integer multiples of T/2 = π/ω′. Continuous lines corresponds to ∆x = 50 nm, while dashed...

FIG. 9: Entanglement in the presence of Marko- vian trap fluctuations Logarithmic negativity over time for different diffusion constants D. Here, D is measured in units of ℏλ and for D = 1ℏλ, the entanglement rate Γent. = ∂tEN is precisely equal to the decoherence rate. Consequently, for larger diffusion, no entanglement is ob- servable, as seen in the inset plot.

FIG. 10: Entanglement dynamics in a GIE setup in the presence of a quantum dynamical shield. Results are shown for delocalized superconducting lead particles interacting with the shield via magnetic-dipole interactions prepared in the parallel orientation (θ = 0), for which the gravitational signal is most robust against noise. The parameters from Tab. I were used with a Niobium shield of size ds = 2 µm and rs = 1 cm. If the particle–shield couplings are dominated by Casimir interactions instead, the corresponding dynamics are shown in Fig. 16. a, Entanglement dynamics for Gaussian states (top) and two-level cat-states (bottom) with the shield dynamics restricted to the dominant (1, 1)-mode...
